nodes.name: Famous Mathematicians
nodes.description: Key mathematicians and their groundbreaking contributions

items.prompt: Which ancient Greek mathematician, active around 300 BCE in Alexandria, is called the 'Father of Geometry' and wrote *Elements*, the most influential mathematics textbook in history?
items.explanation:
Active c. 300 BCE in Ptolemaic Alexandria, Egypt
*Elements* was used as a textbook for over 2,000 years — second only to the Bible in editions printed
Systematized geometry using axioms and logical deduction, establishing the axiomatic method
His fifth postulate (the parallel postulate) spawned centuries of debate and led to non-Euclidean geometry
Almost nothing is known about his personal life; even his birth and death dates are uncertain
Allegedly told Ptolemy I there was 'no royal road to geometry' when asked for a shortcut

items.prompt: Which ancient Greek philosopher and mathematician, born on the island of Samos around 570 BCE, founded a secretive religious brotherhood and is best known for the theorem relating the sides of a right triangle?
items.explanation:
Born c. 570 BCE on Samos; founded a commune-like school in Croton, southern Italy
The Pythagorean theorem (a² + b² = c²) was known to Babylonians 1,000 years earlier, but his school may have proved it
The Pythagorean brotherhood was part math school, part religious cult — members were sworn to secrecy
Believed 'all is number' — that mathematics was the basis of all reality
Legend: a follower (Hippasus) was drowned for proving √2 is irrational, contradicting Pythagorean philosophy
None of his writings survive; everything attributed to him comes from later followers
Also credited with discovering the mathematical ratios underlying musical harmony

items.prompt: Which ancient Greek mathematician and inventor from Syracuse, killed by a Roman soldier during the siege of 212 BCE, discovered the principle of buoyancy and reportedly shouted 'Eureka!' upon solving a problem in his bath?
items.explanation:
Born c. 287 BCE in Syracuse, Sicily — greatest mathematician of antiquity
Discovered the principle of buoyancy (Archimedes' principle) while investigating a golden crown's purity
Calculated pi to between 3 10/71 and 3 1/7 using inscribed and circumscribed polygons
Invented the Archimedes' screw for raising water, still used today
'Give me a lever long enough and a fulcrum on which to place it, and I shall move the world'
Killed in 212 BCE during the Roman siege of Syracuse; reportedly told the soldier 'Do not disturb my circles'
Anticipated integral calculus nearly 2,000 years before Newton and Leibniz

items.prompt: Which English mathematician and physicist, born in 1642, co-invented calculus, formulated the laws of motion and universal gravitation, and wrote *Principia Mathematica*?
items.explanation:
Born December 25, 1642 (Old Style) in Woolsthorpe, Lincolnshire, England
Co-invented calculus independently of Leibniz in the mid-1660s; the priority dispute lasted decades
*Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica* (1687) laid the foundation for classical mechanics
Also made foundational contributions to optics, including the reflecting telescope and prism experiments
Lucasian Professor of Mathematics at Cambridge — the same chair later held by Stephen Hawking
Spent more time on alchemy and biblical chronology than on mathematics or physics
Warden and Master of the Royal Mint; oversaw England's great recoinage and prosecuted counterfeiters
Died in 1727; reportedly a virgin his entire life

items.prompt: Which German polymath independently invented calculus around the same time as Newton, introduced the notation dy/dx and the integral sign ∫, and is also known for his philosophical concept of monads?
items.explanation:
Born 1646 in Leipzig, Germany; died 1716 in Hanover
Independently developed calculus in the 1670s; his notation (dy/dx, ∫) became standard, not Newton's
The Newton–Leibniz priority dispute was one of the most bitter in the history of science
Invented binary arithmetic (base 2), the foundation of modern computing
Also a philosopher: developed the concept of monads and argued this is 'the best of all possible worlds' (satirized by Voltaire in *Candide*)
Designed one of the first mechanical calculators (the Stepped Reckoner)
Died largely forgotten; his funeral was attended only by his personal secretary

items.prompt: Which Swiss mathematician, the most prolific in history with over 800 papers, introduced the notation e, i, π, Σ, and f(x), and discovered the formula e^(iπ) + 1 = 0?
items.explanation:
Born 1707 in Basel, Switzerland; died 1783 in St. Petersburg, Russia
Most prolific mathematician ever — published over 800 papers and books; output continued even after going blind
Introduced or popularized the notation e, i, π, Σ, f(x), and Δ
Euler's identity (e^(iπ) + 1 = 0) is called the most beautiful equation in mathematics
Solved the Seven Bridges of Königsberg problem (1736), founding graph theory
Worked in virtually every branch of mathematics: calculus, number theory, topology, mechanics, optics
Lost sight in his right eye by age 31, became nearly totally blind by 59, but his output increased
Supported by the courts of Frederick the Great (Berlin) and Catherine the Great (St. Petersburg)

items.prompt: Which German mathematician, called the 'Prince of Mathematicians,' made foundational contributions to number theory, statistics, and electromagnetism, and as a child famously summed the integers from 1 to 100 almost instantly?
items.explanation:
Born 1777 in Braunschweig, Germany; died 1855 in Göttingen
Called 'Princeps mathematicorum' (Prince of Mathematicians) — one of the greatest of all time
As a schoolboy, instantly summed 1+2+...+100 = 5,050 by pairing numbers (1+100, 2+99, etc.)
*Disquisitiones Arithmeticae* (1801) founded modern number theory; written when he was 21
The Gaussian (normal/bell curve) distribution is named for him — foundational in statistics
Also contributed to astronomy: predicted the orbit of the asteroid Ceres from minimal observations
The gauss (unit of magnetic flux density) is named after him
Kept many discoveries unpublished; when others published results, he often said 'I had that already'

items.prompt: Which 17th-century French lawyer and amateur mathematician is best known for a marginal note claiming to have proved that a^n + b^n = c^n has no integer solutions for n > 2, a claim that took 358 years to verify?
items.explanation:
Born 1601 in Beaumont-de-Lomagne, France; died 1665 — a lawyer by profession, mathematician by passion
Fermat's Last Theorem: his margin note said 'I have discovered a truly marvelous proof, which this margin is too narrow to contain'
The theorem wasn't proved until 1995 by Andrew Wiles, 358 years later
Co-founded probability theory with Blaise Pascal through correspondence on gambling problems (1654)
Pioneered analytic geometry independently of Descartes
Fermat's Little Theorem (not the Last Theorem) is fundamental in number theory and modern cryptography
Called the 'Prince of Amateurs' — never held an academic position in mathematics

items.prompt: Which French mathematician, physicist, and religious philosopher, who died at age 39, co-founded probability theory, built one of the first mechanical calculators, and has a famous triangular array of numbers named after him?
items.explanation:
Born 1623 in Clermont-Ferrand, France; died 1662 in Paris at age 39
Pascal's triangle arranges binomial coefficients in a triangle; known in China centuries earlier (Yang Hui's triangle)
Co-founded probability theory with Fermat through correspondence about gambling (1654)
Built the Pascaline, one of the first mechanical calculators, at age 19 to help his father with tax calculations
The pascal (Pa) is the SI unit of pressure, named after his experiments with atmospheric pressure and vacuums
Pascal's wager: argued it's rational to believe in God because the potential payoff is infinite
After a religious experience in 1654, largely abandoned mathematics for theology; wrote *Pensées*

items.prompt: Which French philosopher and mathematician, famous for the statement 'I think, therefore I am,' invented the coordinate system that uses x and y axes to plot equations on a plane?
items.explanation:
Born 1596 in La Haye en Touraine, France; died 1650 in Stockholm, Sweden
'Cogito, ergo sum' ('I think, therefore I am') — foundation of modern Western philosophy
Invented the Cartesian coordinate system, bridging algebra and geometry (analytic geometry)
Published *La Géométrie* (1637) as an appendix to *Discourse on the Method*
The convention of using x, y, z for unknowns and a, b, c for constants comes from Descartes
Moved to the Dutch Republic for intellectual freedom; spent 20 years there
Died of pneumonia in Stockholm after Queen Christina of Sweden demanded 5 AM philosophy lessons in a freezing library

items.prompt: Which medieval Italian mathematician, who studied under Arab mathematicians in North Africa, introduced Hindu-Arabic numerals to Europe and described the sequence 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13... in his 1202 book *Liber Abaci*?
items.explanation:
Born c. 1170 in Pisa, Italy; died c. 1250
Real name Leonardo Bonacci; 'Fibonacci' (son of Bonacci) was coined by a 19th-century historian
*Liber Abaci* (1202) introduced Hindu-Arabic numerals (0–9) to Europe, replacing Roman numerals
The Fibonacci sequence (1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13...) appeared as a solution to a rabbit-breeding problem
Consecutive Fibonacci ratios converge to the golden ratio (φ ≈ 1.618)
The sequence appears in nature: sunflower spirals, pinecone scales, nautilus shells
Most important mathematician of the Middle Ages; made European commerce and accounting practical

items.prompt: Which German mathematician, who died of tuberculosis at 39, formulated the hypothesis about the distribution of prime numbers that remains the most important unsolved problem in mathematics, and whose geometry underpins Einstein's general relativity?
items.explanation:
Born 1826 in Breselenz, Germany; died 1866 in Selasca, Italy, of tuberculosis at age 39
The Riemann hypothesis (1859) concerns the distribution of prime numbers — still unsolved, with a $1 million Millennium Prize
Riemannian geometry describes curved spaces and became the mathematical foundation of Einstein's general relativity
His habilitation lecture (1854) on the foundations of geometry revolutionized the field in a single talk
The Riemann integral is the standard definition of integration taught in calculus courses
Despite dying young and publishing only a handful of papers, he transformed multiple branches of mathematics
Son of a poor Lutheran pastor; initially studied theology before switching to mathematics

items.prompt: Which German mathematician, born in St. Petersburg, founded set theory and proved that there are different sizes of infinity, a concept so controversial it drove conflict with contemporaries and contributed to his mental breakdowns?
items.explanation:
Born 1845 in St. Petersburg, Russia; died 1918 in a sanatorium in Halle, Germany
Founded set theory and proved that infinities come in different sizes (cardinalities)
Proved the real numbers are 'more infinite' than the natural numbers using his diagonal argument (1891)
His continuum hypothesis asks whether there's an infinity between the integers and the reals — proved independent of standard axioms
Fiercely opposed by Leopold Kronecker, who called him a 'corruptor of youth' and blocked his career
Suffered repeated mental breakdowns and spent his final years in a sanatorium
David Hilbert defended him: 'No one shall expel us from the paradise that Cantor has created'

items.prompt: Which English mathematician and cryptanalyst, whose work breaking the Enigma code at Bletchley Park helped shorten World War II, formalized the concept of computation with a theoretical machine and is considered the father of computer science?
items.explanation:
Born 1912 in London; died 1954 in Wilmslow, Cheshire
The Turing machine (1936) is the foundational model of computation — defines what is and isn't computable
Led the team at Bletchley Park that broke the German Enigma cipher during WWII, estimated to have shortened the war by 2+ years
Proposed the Turing test (1950): a machine is 'intelligent' if a human can't distinguish its responses from a person's
Prosecuted in 1952 for homosexuality (then a crime in the UK); chose chemical castration over prison
Died of cyanide poisoning in 1954, ruled a suicide — possibly accidental
Received a royal pardon in 2013; his face appears on the Bank of England £50 note since 2021
items.cardFront: Turing machine, Enigma, father of CS

links.text: Turing machine, Enigma, father of CS

items.prompt: Which English mathematician, the daughter of the poet Lord Byron, is credited with writing the first computer algorithm in her notes on Charles Babbage's Analytical Engine in the 1840s?
items.explanation:
Born 1815 in London; died 1852 of uterine cancer at age 36
Daughter of the poet Lord Byron, whom she never knew — Byron left when she was a month old
Wrote extensive notes on Babbage's Analytical Engine (1843), including what is considered the first computer program
Her 'Note G' described an algorithm for the engine to compute Bernoulli numbers
Recognized that computers could go beyond pure calculation to create music and art — a visionary insight for the 1840s
The programming language Ada (1980), commissioned by the U.S. Department of Defense, is named after her
Ada Lovelace Day (second Tuesday of October) celebrates women in STEM

items.prompt: Which German mathematician, whom Einstein called 'the most significant creative mathematical genius thus far produced since the higher education of women began,' proved the theorem linking symmetry to conservation laws in physics?
items.explanation:
Born 1882 in Erlangen, Germany; died 1935 in Bryn Mawr, Pennsylvania
Noether's theorem (1918): every symmetry in physics corresponds to a conservation law — foundational in theoretical physics
Revolutionized abstract algebra; founded the theory of rings and ideals as modern structures
Einstein called her 'the most significant creative mathematical genius thus far produced since the higher education of women began'
Barred from holding a faculty position at Göttingen because she was a woman; Hilbert protested: 'This is a university, not a bathhouse'
Fled Nazi Germany in 1933 (Jewish); took a position at Bryn Mawr College in Pennsylvania
Died unexpectedly at 53 from complications of surgery for an ovarian cyst

items.prompt: Which self-taught Indian mathematician, who sent unsolicited letters to G.H. Hardy at Cambridge, produced thousands of original results in number theory before dying at 32, and is associated with the 'taxicab number' 1729?
items.explanation:
Born 1887 in Erode, Tamil Nadu, India; died 1920 in Kumbakonam at age 32
Largely self-taught; learned advanced mathematics from a single borrowed textbook
Sent unsolicited letters with theorems to G.H. Hardy in 1913; Hardy called them 'the most remarkable I had ever received'
Produced ~3,900 results, many without proofs — most have since been proved correct
1729: when Hardy mentioned arriving in a 'dull' taxi #1729, Ramanujan instantly replied it's the smallest number expressible as the sum of two cubes in two ways (1³+12³ = 9³+10³)
Died young, likely of hepatic amoebiasis (not tuberculosis as long believed)
Dev Patel portrayed him in the 2015 biopic *The Man Who Knew Infinity*

items.prompt: Which French mathematician, killed in a duel at age 20, spent the night before his death frantically writing down mathematical ideas that founded group theory and the branch of algebra named after him?
items.explanation:
Born 1811 in Bourg-la-Reine, France; died 1832 in Paris — killed in a duel at age 20
The night before the duel, he wrote a letter outlining his mathematical ideas, scrawling 'I have not time' in the margins
Founded group theory and Galois theory, which determines when polynomial equations can be solved by radicals
Proved there is no general formula (like the quadratic formula) for polynomials of degree 5 or higher
His work was rejected or lost by the French Academy of Sciences multiple times during his lifetime
A political radical imprisoned twice during the aftermath of the 1830 French revolution
His genius was not recognized until 14 years after his death, when Liouville published his papers in 1846

items.prompt: Which Austrian-American logician proved in 1931 that any sufficiently powerful mathematical system contains true statements that cannot be proved within the system, ending the dream of a complete, consistent foundation for all mathematics?
items.explanation:
Born 1906 in Brünn, Austria-Hungary (now Brno, Czech Republic); died 1978 in Princeton, New Jersey
Gödel's incompleteness theorems (1931): in any consistent formal system capable of basic arithmetic, there are true statements that cannot be proved
Shattered Hilbert's program to establish complete, consistent foundations for all mathematics
Close friend of Albert Einstein at Princeton's Institute for Advanced Study; they walked home together daily
Became a U.S. citizen in 1948; told the judge he'd found a logical flaw in the Constitution that could allow a dictatorship
Suffered from extreme paranoia and fear of being poisoned; would only eat food prepared by his wife Adele
Died of starvation in 1978 after Adele was hospitalized and could no longer cook for him

items.prompt: Which American mathematician, the subject of the 2001 film *A Beautiful Mind*, won the 1994 Nobel Prize in Economics for his foundational work on game theory, including the equilibrium concept that bears his name?
items.explanation:
Born 1928 in Bluefield, West Virginia; died 2015 in a taxi crash on the New Jersey Turnpike
The Nash equilibrium (1950): a game theory solution where no player benefits from unilaterally changing strategy
Won the Nobel Memorial Prize in Economics in 1994, shared with Harsanyi and Selten
Diagnosed with paranoid schizophrenia in 1959; spent years in and out of psychiatric hospitals
Russell Crowe portrayed him in *A Beautiful Mind* (2001), which won Best Picture
Awarded the Abel Prize in 2015 — killed in a taxi crash days later, returning from the ceremony in Norway
His wife Alicia was also killed in the same crash; neither was wearing a seatbelt

items.prompt: Which mathematician and philosopher of late antiquity, head of the Neoplatonic school in Alexandria around 400 CE, was murdered by a Christian mob and is considered the first notable female mathematician in recorded history?
items.explanation:
Born c. 350–370 CE in Alexandria, Egypt; died 415 CE
First female mathematician whose life is well-documented; taught mathematics, astronomy, and philosophy
Head of the Neoplatonic school in Alexandria; students traveled from across the Mediterranean to study with her
Murdered by a mob of Christians in 415 CE, likely for political reasons — caught between the Roman prefect and Bishop Cyril
Edited and wrote commentaries on major mathematical works, including Ptolemy's *Almagest*
Rachel Weisz portrayed her in the film *Agora* (2009)
Her death is sometimes used to mark the end of classical antiquity's intellectual tradition in Alexandria

items.prompt: Which British mathematician, working in secret for seven years at Princeton, proved Fermat's Last Theorem in 1995, solving a problem that had remained unproven for 358 years?
items.explanation:
Born 1953 in Cambridge, England
Proved Fermat's Last Theorem — first announced the proof on June 23, 1993, at a Cambridge lecture
A gap was found in September 1993; corrected with Richard Taylor by September 1994, published 1995
Worked in secret for seven years (1986–1993), telling almost no one, including most colleagues
Inspired at age 10 after reading about Fermat's Last Theorem in a library book
Proved the modularity theorem for semistable elliptic curves — the actual mathematical breakthrough
Awarded the Abel Prize in 2016; knighted in 2000
Ineligible for the Fields Medal (over 40 at time of proof), but received a special silver plaque from the IMU in 1998
